executive_brief:
The threat landscape has shifted from 'AI-assisted' to 'AI-driven' operations, with a terrifying compression of the exploit window. We are seeing the emergence of 'vulnerability vending machines' and LLM-developed botnets (TuxBot v3) that can identify and weaponize flaws in minutes, not days. This acceleration has left traditional patch management cycles obsolete, as evidenced by the record-breaking 622 CVEs in Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday and the immediate, near-simultaneous exploitation of SharePoint and SonicWall zero-days. 

Beyond the software, the attack surface has expanded into 'embodied AI' and the deep supply chain. From poisoned open-weight AI models to trojanized npm packages (AsyncAPI) and the abuse of OAuth trust relationships (ShinyHunters), attackers are bypassing the perimeter entirely. The big picture is a crisis of trust: we can no longer trust signed binaries, trusted OAuth partners, or even the AI agents we deploy to help us secure our networks. The urgency is no longer just about patching; it is about implementing architectural resilience that assumes the identity and the agent are already compromised.

threat_stories.headline: The AI-Accelerated Exploit Cycle: From Discovery to Domain Admin in Minutes
threat_stories.narrative:
The traditional 'race to patch' has been replaced by a sprint where the attackers have a head start. This week, we witnessed the practical application of AI-powered vulnerability discovery. Researchers demonstrated 'vulnerability vending machines' that autonomously find and exploit WordPress flaws [172], while the 'Patriot Bait' actor used AI to spin up a full C2 botnet in just six minutes [133]. This isn't theoretical; it's operational. 

This acceleration is most visible in the 'Patch Tuesday' carnage. Microsoft released a record 622 patches [101], but the window for defense is closing. CISA was forced to issue emergency mandates for SharePoint [180] and FortiSandbox [263] flaws because threat actors are now using AI to diff patches and weaponize them almost instantly. The 'wp2shell' WordPress core flaw [314] followed a similar trajectory, moving from disclosure to public exploit availability in hours, enabling mass automated takeovers [319].

Furthermore, AI is now being used to build the malware itself. TuxBot v3 [136, 210] is a prime example of an LLM-assisted IoT botnet. While some of the AI-generated code contains 'hallucinated' bugs—such as mismatched XOR keys—the core infection flow is 70% functional, proving that 'good enough' AI code is sufficient to maintain a global botnet.

timeline.event: Microsoft releases record 622 Patch Tuesday fixes
timeline.significance: Sets the stage for massive patch-diffing efforts by threat actors.

timeline.event: CISA adds SonicWall and SharePoint flaws to KEV
timeline.significance: Confirms that zero-days are being exploited faster than agencies can patch.
Demonstrates the immediate transition from flaw discovery to public exploit.
Enables mass-scale automated exploitation of the web's most popular CMS.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Every organization running WordPress, SharePoint, or SonicWall appliances is at immediate risk of unauthenticated RCE.
threat_stories.action_required: Shift from 'Patch Tuesday' mentality to 'Continuous Remediation.' Deploy WAF rules to block known RCE patterns for WordPress/SharePoint and isolate all SonicWall SMA appliances until patched.

threat_stories.headline: The 'ClickFix' Pandemic: Social Engineering's New Front Line
threat_stories.narrative:
A sophisticated new social engineering trend dubbed 'ClickFix' is bypassing macOS and Windows security by tricking users into becoming their own attackers. Instead of delivering a file, attackers use fake 'verification' or 'fix-it' pages (often mimicking Cloudflare or system errors) that instruct users to copy a string of text and paste it directly into their Terminal or PowerShell [248, 320].

This technique is the primary delivery vector for a new wave of high-impact stealers. The 'ClickLock' malware on macOS [242, 261] takes this a step further: if a user refuses to provide their password, the malware kills every visible application every 210ms, effectively holding the UI hostage until the user complies. Simultaneously, the 'OkoBot' framework [123, 181, 252] is using these lures to deploy 20 different payloads targeting cryptocurrency seed phrases and browser tokens.

What makes ClickFix dangerous is that it bypasses Gatekeeper and other file-based scanners because the 'malware' is just a command the user willingly executes. It transforms the user's administrative privileges into a weapon against the system.

timeline.significance: First observation of the 'app-killing' extortion tactic.

timeline.event: OkoBot framework identified targeting crypto wallets
timeline.significance: Shows the shift toward highly specialized financial theft via ClickFix.
Alerts the community to the 'Terminal paste' social engineering vector.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Specifically targets macOS power users and crypto-asset holders. Bypasses traditional EDR that focuses on file signatures.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement strict Terminal/PowerShell execution policies. Deploy EDR alerts for any shell process spawning from a browser or executing commands containing 'curl | sh' or 'iwr | iex' patterns.

threat_stories.headline: The Identity Trust Collapse: OAuth and AI Agent Abuse
threat_stories.narrative:
We are seeing a systemic failure in how we trust third-party identities. The 'ShinyHunters' group has spent a year compromising Salesforce environments not by hacking the platform, but by abusing overly permissive OAuth trust relationships [54, 69]. By compromising a third-party vendor with a trusted OAuth connection, they move laterally into corporate data stores without ever triggering a 'failed login' alert.

This trust collapse extends to the new frontier of AI agents. The 'ClaudeBleed' vulnerability [100, 171, 253] allows rogue browser extensions to hijack the Claude for Chrome extension, simulating user clicks to read Gmail, Calendar, and Drive data. Even more concerning is the 'PromptFiction' flaw [144], where hidden prompts in the Claude Desktop app can lead to RCE on the host system.

When we connect AI agents to our SaaS tools, we aren't just adding a feature; we are adding a high-privilege, non-human user that can be manipulated via prompt injection or session hijacking. The risk radius has exploded because the AI agent now has the 'keys to the kingdom' across multiple platforms.

timeline.event: Microsoft maps year-long ShinyHunters Salesforce campaign
timeline.significance: Reveals the long-term viability of OAuth-based lateral movement.
Highlights the risk of 'agentic' permissions in the browser.

timeline.event: PromptFiction flaw identified in Claude Desktop
timeline.significance: Connects AI prompt manipulation directly to host-level RCE.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Severe. Affects any enterprise using Salesforce with third-party integrations or deploying AI coding/productivity agents.
threat_stories.action_required: Conduct an immediate audit of all OAuth connected apps in Salesforce and M365. Revoke any stale or overly broad permissions. Disable 'Act without asking' modes in all AI extensions.

actor_profiles.name: ShinyHunters
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Conducting long-term campaigns against Salesforce environments by abusing OAuth trust relationships with third-party vendors [54, 69].

actor_profiles.name: UAT-11795
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Distributing trojanized NSIS installers for Zoom and Webex to deploy Starland RAT and WLDR memory-only implants [219, 278].

actor_profiles.name: Scattered Spider
actor_profiles.recent_activity: High-profile attacks on critical infrastructure, including Transport for London (TfL) and Qantas [186, 221, 249].

actor_profiles.name: bandcampro
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Using jailbroken Google Gemini to automate the creation and deployment of C2 servers in under 6 minutes [79, 177].

actor_profiles.name: CylindricalCanine
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Linked to the DigiCert breach and the theft of high-value code-signing certificates [292].

critical_vulnerabilities.description: A critical deserialization flaw allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to execute arbitrary code (RCE).
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply July 2026 Patch Tuesday updates immediately.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Server-side request forgery (SSRF) in the Work Place interface enabling unauthenticated RCE.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade to platform-hotfix version 12.4.3-03453 or higher.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper input validation allowing unauthenticated account takeover via network access (deep link abuse).
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Zoom Desktop Client to version 7.0.0 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Out-of-bounds write vulnerability allowing authenticated attackers to cause memory corruption and data exposure.
Heap buffer overflow in worker process via crafted HTTP requests, potentially leading to RCE.

hunting_leads.title: Detecting 'ClickFix' Terminal Execution
hunting_leads.context: Attackers are tricking users into pasting malicious scripts into the Terminal. Hunt for suspicious shell activity originating from a browser context.
hunting_leads.query: index=endpoint_logs process_name=bash OR process_name=zsh OR process_name=powershell.exe | search cmd_line="*curl*|*sh*" OR cmd_line="*iwr*|*iex*"

hunting_leads.title: Identifying OAuth Lateral Movement in Salesforce
hunting_leads.context: ShinyHunters are abusing third-party OAuth tokens to access Salesforce data without standard login events.
hunting_leads.query: index=salesforce_logs event_type=OAuthTokenUsage | stats count by client_id, user_id | where count > 1000 AND user_id="admin"

hunting_leads.title: Hunting for 'TuxBot v3' IoT C2
hunting_leads.context: The LLM-developed botnet uses specific C2 IPs for its DDoS-for-hire operations.

top_vulnerabilities: {'summary': 'Unauthenticated remote access to RabbitMQ broker OAuth client secret', 'severity': 'critical', 'affected_products': ['RabbitMQ'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Restrict network access to the RabbitMQ management interface', 'Implement IP whitelisting for broker APIs', 'Rotate OAuth client secrets immediately'], 'references': ['https://www.securityweek.com/rabbitmq-vulnerability-threatens-enterprise-systems'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': "An unauthenticated vulnerability in RabbitMQ allows remote attackers to extract the broker's confidential OAuth client secret. Compromise of this secret enables attackers to fully take control of the message broker, potentially leading to data exfiltration, message manipulation, or complete service disruption.", 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'unproven', 'availability_impact': 'high', 'remediation': 'Apply the latest vendor security patches for RabbitMQ. Rotate all compromised OAuth client secrets immediately. Restrict network access to the RabbitMQ management and API ports to trusted IPs only.', 'exploit_available': 'unknown', 'cve_id': '', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'none', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-07-13', 'discovery_date': ''}

{'summary': 'Unauthenticated information disclosure via obsolete management endpoint exposing OAuth client secrets.', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['RabbitMQ'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': 8.7, 'workarounds': ['Restrict network access to the RabbitMQ management interface to trusted networks only.'], 'references': ['https://www.csoonline.com/article/rabbitmq-flaws-expose-oauth-secrets-risk-complete-takeover-of-the-broker'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': "CVE-2026-57219 allows unauthenticated attackers with network access to RabbitMQ's management interface to retrieve the broker's OAuth client secret by querying the obsolete 'GET/api/auth' endpoint. In deployments using confidential OAuth clients (e.g., Microsoft Entra ID, Auth0, Keycloak, UAA), attackers can exchange the leaked secret for an administrator token, enabling complete control over the broker, including accessing/modifying messages, creating users, and altering configurations.", 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'unproven', 'availability_impact': 'high', 'remediation': 'Upgrade to patched versions (3.13.15, 4.0.20, 4.1.11, or 4.2.6). Rotate any exposed OAuth client secrets immediately after patching. Ensure the management interface is never exposed to untrusted networks.', 'exploit_available': 'unknown', 'cve_id': 'CVE-2026-57219', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'none', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-07-13', 'discovery_date': ''}

{'summary': 'Authorization bypass in passive queue and exchange declaration operations allowing metadata reconnaissance.', 'severity': 'medium', 'affected_products': ['RabbitMQ'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Isolate tenants into separate virtual hosts until patching can be completed.'], 'references': ['https://www.csoonline.com/article/rabbitmq-flaws-expose-oauth-secrets-risk-complete-takeover-of-the-broker'], 'integrity_impact': 'none', 'description': "CVE-2026-57221 is an authorization bypass affecting RabbitMQ's passive queue and exchange declaration operations. The permission check is skipped, allowing accounts with valid credentials but no assigned permissions to discover whether queues and exchanges exist and retrieve metadata such as message counts and active consumers. This facilitates reconnaissance in shared environments without exposing message contents or allowing tampering.", 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'low', 'exploit_maturity': 'unproven', 'availability_impact': 'none', 'remediation': 'Upgrade to a patched release of RabbitMQ.', 'exploit_available': 'unknown', 'cve_id': 'CVE-2026-57221', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'none', 'scope': 'unchanged', 'confidentiality_impact': 'low', 'published_date': '2026-07-13', 'discovery_date': ''}

{'summary': 'AI coding agents can be manipulated by hidden instructions embedded in image files referenced by repository policy files, leading to unauthorized secret exfiltration.', 'severity': 'high', 'affected_products': ['Cursor', 'Antigravity', 'Claude Code', 'AI coding agents with multimodal capabilities'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Disable AI agent access to sensitive directories and credential files', 'Manually inspect all image and document attachments in pull requests', 'Use AI coding tools with strict, verified guardrails (e.g., Claude Code)', 'Avoid referencing external or unverified image files in AGENTS.md or policy files'], 'references': ['Malwarebytes: Ghostcommit attack hides malicious AI instructions in images', 'ASSET Research Group: Ghostcommit PoC'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': "The Ghostcommit proof-of-concept demonstrates a multimodal prompt injection vulnerability in AI-assisted code review workflows. Attackers embed malicious instructions within standard image files (e.g., PNG) and reference them in repository policy files like AGENTS.md. When an AI coding agent processes a pull request or routine task, it reads the image, interprets the hidden instructions, and executes them. This can lead to the agent reading sensitive configuration files, extracting secrets, and writing them back into the codebase in an obfuscated form. The vulnerability highlights that the AI tool's harness (which dictates file loading, trust conventions, and guardrails) plays a more critical role in exploitation than the underlying LLM. Tools like Cursor and Antigravity were shown to follow the instructions, while Claude Code's harness refused due to built-in guardrails.", 'attack_vector': 'local', 'privileges_required': 'low', 'exploit_maturity': 'proof-of-concept', 'availability_impact': 'none', 'remediation': 'Implement strict access controls for secrets and configuration files. Configure AI coding tool harnesses with explicit guardrails against reading untrusted multimodal inputs. Enforce manual or automated inspection of non-text attachments in pull requests. Audit and restrict permissions granted to AI agents within development environments.', 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': 'N/A', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'required', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '2026-07-13', 'discovery_date': ''}

{'summary': 'Large-scale exploitation campaign targeting multiple unpatched CMS platforms and WordPress plugins to deploy webshells via unauthenticated file upload, RCE, SSRF, and deserialization flaws.', 'severity': 'critical', 'affected_products': ['WordPress (Simple File List, WavePlayer, BerqWP, WPBookit, Ninja Forms, ThemeREX Addons, Breeze Cache, pay-uz, ACF Extended, WPvivid Backup, Gravity Forms, GutenKit/Hunk Companion)', 'Sneeit Framework', 'Craft CMS', 'MaxSite CMS', 'MetInfo CMS', 'Joomla (JCE plugin)'], 'complexity': 'low', 'cvss_score': '', 'workarounds': ['Configure web directories as read-only to prevent webshell deployment', 'Block unnecessary network connections between internet-facing servers and internal corporate systems', 'Monitor for unexpected child processes spawned by the web server'], 'references': ["Australia's Signals Directorate Alert", 'Five Eyes Joint Statement on AI-Accelerated Cyber Operations', 'SecurityAffairs Article (2026-07-13)'], 'integrity_impact': 'high', 'description': 'Attackers are actively scanning for and exploiting known vulnerabilities across 17 CVEs affecting various CMS platforms and WordPress plugins. The flaws primarily allow unauthenticated file upload, remote code execution (RCE), server-side request forgery (SSRF), or deserialization. Successful exploitation leads to webshell deployment, granting persistent remote access to compromised servers. Attackers leverage these footholds for website defacement, credential harvesting, malware distribution, and lateral movement into broader corporate networks.', 'attack_vector': 'network', 'privileges_required': 'none', 'exploit_maturity': 'high', 'availability_impact': 'high', 'remediation': 'Immediately update all affected CMS platforms and plugins to the latest patched versions. Enable auto-patching where rollback is feasible. If compromised, do not simply delete the webshell; trace initial exploitation via logs, check for lateral movement or new accounts, and restore from a known-good backup before restoring service.', 'exploit_available': 'yes', 'cve_id': 'CVE-2025-34085, CVE-2020-36847, CVE-2025-12057, CVE-2025-7443, CVE-2025-7852, CVE-2026-0740, CVE-2026-1969, CVE-2026-3844, CVE-2026-31843, CVE-2025-13486, CVE-2025-6389, CVE-2026-1357, CVE-2025-12352, CVE-2024-9234, CVE-2025-32432, CVE-2026-3395, CVE-2026-29014, CVE-2026-48907', 'cvss_vector': '', 'user_interaction': 'none', 'scope': 'changed', 'confidentiality_impact': 'high', 'published_date': '', 'discovery_date': ''}
statistics.key_changes: Shift from software-based exploits to 'identity-based' trust abuse (OAuth) and 'user-assisted' execution (ClickFix).
emerging_trends: LLM-generated malware with 'hallucinated' but functional code
Attackers targeting 'Embodied AI' (Robotics) and AI agent permissions
Massive surge in Patch Tuesday volume (622 CVEs)
declining_threats: Traditional file-based phishing (replaced by ClickFix/Terminal lures)
Slow-burn vulnerability research (replaced by AI-driven diffing)
